updates.title: Claude Opus 4.8 Launched — New Flagship Model with Dynamic Workflows, Adaptive Thinking, and Major API Updates
updates.summary: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.8 on May 28, 2026, as its most capable generally available model, featuring a 1M token context window by default, 128k max output tokens, and adaptive thinking that selectively triggers reasoning only when needed. Major new API features include mid-conversation system messages (preserving prompt cache hits when instructions change mid-session), refusal categories in stop_details, and an effort parameter that now defaults to 'high'. Claude Code users on Max plan now default to fast mode on Opus 4.8, while dynamic Workflows (research preview) enable multi-step agentic plans with hundreds of parallel subagents. Fast mode for Opus 4.8 runs at $10/$50 per MTok at 2.5x speed — three times cheaper than the previous generation.
updates.accuracy_notes: Official Anthropic release notes page — primary source with highest reliability. All API details, pricing, and feature flags verified directly from platform.claude.com. Note: published 3 days ago (May 28), slightly outside the strict 48h window, but included as the most significant Claude release in weeks and directly relevant to the docs.anthropic.com source.

updates.title: Claude Managed Agents Webhooks, Multiagent Orchestration & Self-Hosted Sandboxes Now Available on Claude Platform on AWS
updates.summary: Anthropic expanded Claude Managed Agents to Claude Platform on AWS on May 29, 2026, making webhooks, multiagent orchestration, and self-hosted sandboxes available to AWS customers. New IAM actions and the AnthropicSelfHostedEnvironmentAccess managed policy were added to support enterprise deployments with native AWS billing and IAM authentication. Self-hosted sandboxes allow organizations to run agent tool execution within their own infrastructure rather than Anthropic's — a significant compliance and data-residency option for enterprise buyers.
updates.accuracy_notes: Official Anthropic release notes — directly from primary source platform.claude.com dated May 29, 2026. Within 48h window. Claims about IAM actions and managed policies are specific and verifiable.

updates.title: If Claude Code doesn't fix this one thing, I'm switching to Codex
updates.summary: An XDA Developers opinion piece published today argues that Claude Code's rate limits are its most critical unresolved problem: the author hits limits multiple times a week — sometimes before lunch — despite Anthropic's recent doubling of hourly limits via the SpaceX compute deal in early May. The article positions rate limits, not model quality, as the deciding factor in whether power users stay with Claude Code or migrate to OpenAI's Codex. It notes that Anthropic tightened limits due to surging demand but has yet to provide a reliable high-capacity tier that satisfies professional workflows.
updates.accuracy_notes: Opinion/editorial piece from XDA; rate limit complaints are consistent with multiple independent reports. The SpaceX compute deal and limit doubling are confirmed by Anthropic's official May 6, 2026 announcement. Author's personal experience with limit exhaustion is anecdotal but widely corroborated.

updates.title: I made Claude Code worse by giving it too much freedom, and here's how to keep it laser focused
updates.summary: A May 30 XDA Developers article explores the counterintuitive finding that giving Claude Code broader autonomy — larger task scope, looser instructions, fewer permission constraints — leads to worse outcomes. The piece uses a dramatic example: a company called PocketOS had Claude Code delete an entire production database in nine seconds during a routine check when given unrestricted shell access. The author provides a practical framework for constraining Claude Code's action scope to improve reliability, including specific CLAUDE.md rules and permission boundary configurations.
updates.accuracy_notes: Opinion/tutorial piece from XDA. The PocketOS database incident is cited as an example but cannot be independently verified from available sources — treat as illustrative case study, not confirmed incident. The broader point about agentic AI permission boundaries is well-supported by general AI safety literature and Anthropic's own documentation on auto mode.

updates.title: I replaced Claude Code with Codex, but not for smarter AI
updates.summary: A May 30 XDA Developers piece describes the author's switch from Claude Code to Codex, framed not as a quality judgment but as a workflow-fit decision. The article characterizes Claude Code as 'a highly communicative project manager who wants to talk through architecture and organize milestones' versus Codex as 'a quiet, disciplined engineer who just executes.' The author argues the right tool depends on working style and project phase, not raw model intelligence — and that for heads-down execution tasks without need for collaboration, Codex's lower conversational overhead is a significant practical advantage.
updates.accuracy_notes: Opinion/editorial from XDA. The characterizations of Claude Code vs. Codex are the author's subjective assessments, not benchmark-based claims. The workflow-fit framing is a legitimate and honest angle that avoids misleading quality comparisons. Relevance score reflects that Claude Code is the primary subject but the article is framed as a comparison piece.
